title: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Neurology, Neurosurgery
objectives: Apply diagnostic algorithm including CT and LP timing
Understand complications including vasospasm and rebleeding
Manage acute SAH with blood pressure control and aneurysm securing
Prevent and treat delayed cerebral ischemia

vignette.text:
A 52-year-old woman presents with sudden onset severe headache that began 4 hours ago while at rest. She describes it as 'the worst headache of my life' reaching maximum intensity within seconds. She had brief loss of consciousness and one episode of vomiting.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, smoking 1 pack/day

Medications: Lisinopril 10mg daily

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.neuro: GCS 14 (E4V4M6), mild neck stiffness, no focal deficits, no papilledema

questions.stem: This patient has sudden severe headache with classic features.
questions.question: What are the clinical features of subarachnoid hemorrhage?

options.text: SAH CLINICAL FEATURES: CLASSIC PRESENTATION: 'Thunderclap' headache - worst headache of life; Sudden onset - maximal intensity within seconds; May occur during exertion or at rest; ASSOCIATED SYMPTOMS: Nausea/vomiting (77%); Neck stiffness (35%, develops over hours); Brief LOC (50%); Photophobia; Seizures (10-25%); SENTINEL HEADACHE: 'Warning leak' in 30-50%; Days to weeks before major SAH; Often dismissed as migraine; PHYSICAL FINDINGS: Meningismus (takes hours to develop); Focal neuro deficits (if from aneurysm compression - e.g., CN III palsy with PComm aneurysm); Altered consciousness; Subhyaloid hemorrhages (fundoscopy); GRADING: Hunt-Hess scale (I-V); Higher grade = worse prognosis

questions.explanation: SAH Clinical Features: CLASSIC: Thunderclap headache - worst of life, maximal intensity within seconds, can occur at rest or with exertion. ASSOCIATED: Nausea/vomiting, neck stiffness (develops over hours), brief LOC (50%), photophobia, seizures. SENTINEL HEADACHE in 30-50% days to weeks before major bleed. EXAM: Meningismus (delayed), focal deficits from aneurysm compression (CN III palsy with PComm), altered consciousness. Hunt-Hess grade correlates with prognosis.

questions.stem: You need to confirm the diagnosis.
questions.question: What is the diagnostic approach to suspected SAH?

options.text: CT is 99% sensitive at all time points

SAH DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: STEP 1 - NON-CONTRAST CT HEAD: Sensitivity by timing: <6 hours: ~99% sensitive; 6-12 hours: ~95%; 12-24 hours: ~93%; >24 hours: ~80% (declines significantly); Findings: Hyperdense blood in subarachnoid space, cisterns, sylvian fissures; STEP 2 - IF CT NEGATIVE, LUMBAR PUNCTURE: Wait at least 6 hours from symptom onset (allow xanthochromia to develop); CSF findings: Elevated opening pressure; RBCs that don't clear across tubes (vs traumatic tap); XANTHOCHROMIA (yellow CSF from bilirubin) - KEY finding; STEP 3 - ANGIOGRAPHY (if SAH confirmed): CTA: First-line for aneurysm detection (95%+ sensitivity for ≥3mm); DSA (catheter): Gold standard, allows treatment; MRA: Alternative

questions.explanation: SAH Diagnostic Approach: CT HEAD first - sensitivity ~99% within 6 hours but declines to ~80% after 24 hours. IF CT NEGATIVE → LP (wait 6 hours from onset for xanthochromia to develop). CSF findings: elevated pressure, RBCs that don't clear, XANTHOCHROMIA (key - yellow from bilirubin). IF SAH CONFIRMED → CTA for aneurysm detection (95%+ for ≥3mm), DSA is gold standard and allows treatment.

sources.text: StatPearls: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Powers WJ, et al. Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update. Stroke. 2019;50:e344-e418. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What are the causes of SAH?

options.text: SAH ETIOLOGIES: ANEURYSMAL (85%): Saccular (berry) aneurysms; Common locations: AComm (30%), PComm (25%), MCA (20%); Risk factors: Hypertension, smoking, family history, ADPKD, connective tissue disorders, cocaine; NON-ANEURYSMAL: Perimesencephalic (10%): Benign pattern, often no source found, good prognosis; Trauma; Arteriovenous malformation; Dural AV fistula; Vasculitis; Coagulopathy; Cocaine, amphetamines; Pituitary apoplexy; ANEURYSM CHARACTERISTICS: Size: Larger = higher rupture risk; Location: PComm/AComm higher risk; Multiple aneurysms in 20-30%
Aneurysms only occur at one site
Hypertension is not a risk factor

questions.explanation: SAH Etiology: ANEURYSMAL (85%): Saccular aneurysms at AComm (30%), PComm (25%), MCA (20%). Risk factors include hypertension, smoking, family history, ADPKD, connective tissue disorders, cocaine. NON-ANEURYSMAL includes perimesencephalic (benign pattern), trauma, AVM, vasculitis, coagulopathy. Multiple aneurysms present in 20-30%.

questions.question: What is the acute management of SAH?

options.text: ACUTE SAH MANAGEMENT: AIRWAY/BREATHING: Intubate if GCS ≤8 or airway compromise; Avoid hypoxia and hypercapnia; BLOOD PRESSURE: Goal SBP <160 mmHg UNTIL aneurysm secured; Prevents rebleeding (highest risk in first 24h); Use titratable agents (nicardipine, labetalol); ANEURYSM SECURING (within 24h if possible): Surgical clipping; Endovascular coiling (often preferred); PREVENT REBLEEDING: Bed rest, quiet environment; Antifibrinolytics (TXA) if aneurysm treatment delayed; Short-term use only (<72h) due to ischemic risk; GENERAL: Seizure prophylaxis (controversial, often used acutely); Analgesia (headache); Stool softeners (avoid Valsalva); Antiemetics; Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis; TRANSFER to center with neurovascular expertise

questions.explanation: Acute SAH Management: AIRWAY: Intubate if GCS ≤8. BP CONTROL: SBP <160 until aneurysm secured (prevent rebleeding). SECURE ANEURYSM within 24h if possible via clipping or coiling. PREVENT REBLEEDING: Bed rest, antifibrinolytics short-term if treatment delayed. SUPPORTIVE: Seizure prophylaxis (controversial), analgesia, antiemetics, stool softeners, VTE prophylaxis. TRANSFER to neurovascular center.

questions.stem: On day 5, she develops confusion and left arm weakness.
questions.question: What is vasospasm and delayed cerebral ischemia?

options.text: VASOSPASM & DELAYED CEREBRAL ISCHEMIA: VASOSPASM: Arterial narrowing due to blood products; Occurs in 70% angiographically; TIMING: Onset day 3-4; Peak day 7-10; Resolves by day 14-21; DELAYED CEREBRAL ISCHEMIA (DCI): Clinical deterioration from ischemia; Occurs in 30% of SAH patients; Leading cause of morbidity in SAH survivors; CLINICAL: New focal deficits (weakness, aphasia); Declining level of consciousness; DIAGNOSIS: Clinical (decline in neuro exam); TCD: Increased velocities (MCA >120 cm/s concerning, >200 severe); CTA/DSA: Arterial narrowing; CT perfusion: Ischemic changes; PREVENTION: NIMODIPINE 60mg PO q4h × 21 days (improves outcomes, may not prevent angiographic vasospasm); Euvolemia maintenance; Avoid hypotension

questions.explanation: Vasospasm & DCI: VASOSPASM from blood products causes arterial narrowing in 70%, timing day 3-4 onset, peaks day 7-10, resolves by day 14-21. DCI (clinical ischemia) affects 30% and is leading cause of morbidity. CLINICAL: New deficits, declining consciousness. DIAGNOSIS: TCD (MCA velocity >120 concerning), CTA/DSA, CT perfusion. PREVENTION: NIMODIPINE 60mg PO q4h × 21 days (improves outcomes), euvolemia, avoid hypotension.

questions.stem: TCD shows elevated velocities consistent with vasospasm.
questions.question: How is symptomatic vasospasm/DCI treated?

options.text: DCI TREATMENT: INDUCED HYPERTENSION: First-line for symptomatic vasospasm; Target SBP 180-200 (or 20-40 above baseline); Vasopressors: Phenylephrine or norepinephrine; Only AFTER aneurysm secured; EUVOLEMIA: Avoid hypovolemia; Hypervolemia NOT beneficial (old 'Triple H' abandoned); ENDOVASCULAR: If refractory to medical management; Intra-arterial vasodilators (verapamil, nicardipine); Balloon angioplasty for severe proximal vasospasm; TRIPLE-H THERAPY (Hypertension, Hypervolemia, Hemodilution): Historically used; Now: Only HYPERTENSION component supported; Hypervolemia increases complications without benefit; MONITORING: Neuro exams q1-2h; TCD daily (or more frequent); CT/CTA if clinical change
Triple-H therapy fully supported by evidence

questions.explanation: DCI Treatment: INDUCED HYPERTENSION first-line (SBP 180-200) after aneurysm secured, using phenylephrine or norepinephrine. EUVOLEMIA (NOT hypervolemia - old Triple-H abandoned except hypertension component). ENDOVASCULAR for refractory cases: intra-arterial vasodilators, balloon angioplasty. MONITORING: Frequent neuro exams, daily TCD, imaging if clinical change.

questions.question: What are other major complications of SAH?

options.text: Rebleeding risk is constant over time

SAH COMPLICATIONS: REBLEEDING: Highest risk in first 24 hours; 4% in first 24h, 20% in first 2 weeks; Devastatingly high mortality; Prevention: Early aneurysm securing, BP control; HYDROCEPHALUS: Acute (obstruction from blood); Chronic (decreased CSF absorption); Monitor with serial CT; Treatment: EVD or VP shunt; SEIZURES: 10-25% acutely; Prophylaxis controversial (often used 3-7 days); Long-term AEDs not routinely recommended; CARDIAC: Myocardial stunning, Takotsubo; Arrhythmias (QT prolongation); Troponin elevation; HYPONATREMIA: SIADH vs Cerebral salt wasting; CSW: Volume depleted, needs salt replacement; SIADH: Euvolemic, fluid restrict; DVT/PE: High risk, use prophylaxis; Neurogenic pulmonary edema

questions.explanation: SAH Complications: REBLEEDING highest in first 24h (4%), devastating mortality - prevent with early aneurysm securing. HYDROCEPHALUS acute or chronic, monitor with CT, treat with EVD/shunt. SEIZURES 10-25%, prophylaxis controversial. CARDIAC: Myocardial stunning, Takotsubo, arrhythmias, troponin elevation. HYPONATREMIA: Distinguish SIADH (restrict fluid) from CSW (replace salt and volume). DVT/PE high risk.

sources.text: StatPearls: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Powers WJ, et al. Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update. Stroke. 2019;50:e344-e418. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: CT shows diffuse SAH. CTA reveals 7mm AComm aneurysm.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) DIAGNOSIS CONFIRMED: CT SAH + CTA shows AComm aneurysm; (2) IMMEDIATE MANAGEMENT: Admit to ICU, neurological monitoring; BP control: SBP <160 until aneurysm secured; Analgesia, antiemetics, stool softeners; (3) SECURE ANEURYSM within 24h: Endovascular coiling vs surgical clipping; Discuss with neurovascular team; (4) NIMODIPINE 60mg PO q4h × 21 days (start immediately); (5) AFTER ANEURYSM SECURED: Can liberalize BP; If DCI develops - induced hypertension; (6) MONITOR FOR COMPLICATIONS: Daily TCD (vasospasm days 3-14); Serial CT (hydrocephalus); Neuro exams q1-2h; Sodium levels (SIADH/CSW); Cardiac monitoring; (7) VTE prophylaxis after aneurysm secured; (8) Hunt-Hess grade determines prognosis
BP control not needed after diagnosis

questions.explanation: Complete SAH Management: DIAGNOSIS confirmed with CT + CTA. IMMEDIATE: ICU, BP <160 until aneurysm secured, supportive care. SECURE ANEURYSM within 24h via coiling or clipping. NIMODIPINE 60mg q4h × 21 days started immediately. AFTER SECURING: Liberalize BP, induced hypertension if DCI. MONITOR: Daily TCD days 3-14, serial CT for hydrocephalus, frequent neuro exams, sodium, cardiac. VTE prophylaxis after aneurysm secured. Prognosis correlates with Hunt-Hess grade.

sources.text: StatPearls: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
